seo_title: Solid Bronze Jewelry: Care, Quality & Wear Guide | Moon Raven Designs
meta_description: Discover solid bronze jewelry at Moon Raven Designs. Learn about its durability, tarnish, care, and hypoallergenic properties. Handcrafted on Vancouver Island since 1974.
intro: Bronze is one of humanity’s oldest alloys, born from fire and earth. At Moon Raven Designs, we work with solid bronze—a blend of copper and tin—because it carries warmth, weight, and a living patina that deepens with time. Each piece begins as raw metal, shaped by hand on Vancouver Island, and emerges with a quiet, grounded presence that feels both ancient and new. This is not a metal that stays still; it breathes, shifts, and tells your story.
properties: Solid bronze is dense and substantial—heavier than brass or sterling silver, with a satisfying heft that speaks of permanence. Our bronze is typically 85-90% copper and 10-15% tin, sometimes with small additions of other metals for workability. It arrives with a warm, golden-brown glow that gradually oxidizes into a rich, earthy patina—ranging from deep chocolate to verdigris hints. This tarnish is natural and protective, not a flaw. Bronze is generally safe for most skin, but the high copper content can cause green discoloration on some wearers (especially in warm, moist conditions). This is not an allergic reaction but a harmless chemical reaction; it washes off easily. Bronze is durable and resilient, ideal for rings, cuffs, and pendants that endure daily wear without bending or breaking.
care: To keep your bronze jewelry vibrant, avoid harsh chemicals, chlorine, salt water, and abrasive cleaners. Remove it before swimming, showering, or applying lotions. To clean, simply polish with a soft cloth—our Moon Raven polishing cloth works beautifully. For a deeper clean, use a mild soap and water solution, then dry thoroughly. If you prefer a bright, new finish, use a commercial metal polish (like Brasso) sparingly, but know that the patina will return. To refresh a darkened patina, apply a thin layer of olive oil and buff gently. For structural repairs or stone resetting, bring your piece to a jeweler; bronze can be reworked but requires skill. Store in a dry pouch or box away from other metals to prevent scratching.
who_chooses: Solid bronze appeals to those who value authenticity and imperfection—people who want jewelry that lives with them, not just on them. It suits earthy, minimalist, or bohemian styles and pairs beautifully with leather, wood, and gemstones. Bronze rings and cuffs are favored by those who work with their hands, as the metal develops a unique character over time. This material carries an intent of grounding, resilience, and connection to tradition—a quiet statement that beauty grows with age.

faq.q: Is solid bronze jewelry good quality?
faq.a: Yes. Solid bronze is durable, dense, and long-lasting. It resists bending and cracking, and its patina protects the metal from corrosion. With proper care, bronze jewelry can last for generations.

faq.q: Will solid bronze turn skin green?
faq.a: It can. The copper content in bronze may react with skin acids and moisture, leaving a green mark that washes off easily. This is harmless and not an allergy. Applying a clear sealant or keeping the metal dry can reduce the effect.

faq.q: How do you keep bronze jewelry from tarnishing?
faq.a: Tarnish is natural and part of bronze's appeal. To slow it, store in a dry, airtight container, avoid moisture and chemicals, and polish occasionally with a soft cloth. A thin coat of Renaissance Wax or clear nail polish on the inside of rings can also help.

faq.q: Can you wear bronze jewelry in the shower?
faq.a: We recommend removing bronze jewelry before showering. Soap, shampoo, and hot water accelerate tarnish and can dull the finish. If it gets wet, dry it thoroughly with a soft cloth.

faq.q: What is the difference between solid bronze and brass jewelry?
faq.a: Bronze is primarily copper and tin, while brass is copper and zinc. Bronze has a warmer, redder hue and develops a richer patina. It is also slightly denser and more corrosion-resistant than brass.

Bronze is not hypoallergenic for everyone. The copper content can cause green skin, and some people are sensitive to copper or tin. However, true allergic reactions are rare. If you have metal sensitivities, consider a clear protective coating.
